serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of pork belly with black fungus as a side dish in a Singaporean meal is approximately 150 g, representing a moderate portion of braised pork belly and black fungus alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Pork belly with black fungus — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pork belly with black fungus (cr_id F06100028). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pork belly with black fungus. GI estimated at approximately 25 (low) based on analogous low-carbohydrate braised meat dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Carbohydrate content is very low at 2.7g/100g, predominantly from black fungus and preserved tofu with minimal starch. GL per 150g serving = (25 × 2.7 × 1.5) / 100 ≈ 1.0, confirming negligible glycaemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 212 mg/100g; elevated saturated fat contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 100–120 g per meal and reduce soy sauce and oyster sauce quantities during preparation; trim visible pork belly fat before cooking to lower both sodium and saturated fat intake in line with DASH dietary targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate at 2.7 g/100g with sugar 1.7 g/100g; high protein moderates glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Carbohydrate content is negligible and the high protein content (12.6 g/100g) slows gastric emptying, making this dish suitable for blood glucose management when portion is kept to 80–100 g and no additional sweet sauces are added during preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 6.8 g/100g from pork belly; elevated sodium at 212 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at approximately 6.8 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g cardiovascular threshold; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions (≤80 g), trim fat before cooking, and substitute pork belly with leaner cuts such as pork loin or chicken breast to reduce atherogenic lipid load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid from red meat amplifies inflammatory cytokines; saturated fat exceeds 5 g/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pork belly is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to high arachidonic acid content and saturated fat at approximately 6.8 g/100g; substitute with chicken thigh or tofu to retain the anti-inflammatory benefit of black fungus beta-glucans while reducing pro-inflammatory lipid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork belly with black fungus is a traditional Singaporean Chinese braised dish consisting of slow-cooked pork belly paired with rehydrated black wood ear fungus (Auricularia auricula-judae) and salted preserved tofu (nam yue or white fermented tofu), seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, ginger, and garlic. The dish is characterised by its rich, savoury-umami flavour profile, with the gelatinous and fatty pork belly providing a melt-in-the-mouth texture contrasted by the crunchy, cartilaginous bite of the black fungus. Nutritionally, it provides 231 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 18.9 g (approximately 6.8 g saturated), moderate protein at 12.6 g, and very low carbohydrate at 2.7 g. Sodium is 212 mg/100g, placing it in the borderline-acceptable range for sodium-restricted diets. The black fungus contributes dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g), beta-glucans with immune-modulating properties, and trace minerals. Clinically, the dish is suitable for type 2 diabetes management due to its negligible carbohydrate and glycaemic load, but requires caution for cardiovascular disease and hypertension patients due to elevated saturated fat and sodium. It is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to arachidonic acid content in red meat. Commonly served as a side dish in Singaporean hawker centres and home cooking, often accompanied by steamed white rice.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06100028) at confidence 0.95. This is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from the canonical HPB entry.
notes: Saturated fat value of approximately 6.8 g/100g is estimated from the total fat of 18.9 g/100g using a typical pork belly fatty acid profile (approximately 36% saturated), as the HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this value is used for health context calculations. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients is not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for F06100028 (cr_id: F06100028), and no other approved source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA) provides disaggregated values for this specific braised composite dish: monounsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not available in HPB Foodo for this dish; polyunsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractionation not available in HPB Foodo for this dish; trans_fat_g — trans fat not reported for this dish in HPB Foodo; cholesterol_mg — cholesterol not reported for this dish in HPB Foodo; vitamin_c_mg — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; vitamin_b6_mg — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; vitamin_b12_mcg — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; folate_mcg_dfe — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; vitamin_d_mcg — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; vitamin_e_mg — micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; phosphorus_mg — mineral panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; magnesium_mg — mineral panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; zinc_mg — mineral panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; selenium_mcg — mineral panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish; water_g — moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this dish. All -1 values reflect genuine data unavailability from the primary approved source and were confirmed absent in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the closest analogous braised pork belly composite entry. potassium_mg value of 220 mg/100g is an estimate based on analogous braised pork belly dishes in USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and is consistent with the input record's stated potassium estimate. Dietary tags in the original input incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based'; these have been corrected to false in dietary_flags as the dish contains pork. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish.
